Useful R Stuff

Working with dataframes

janitor - clean dirty data, tabulating tools, adornments

Clean up column names

library(janitor)
# Create a data.frame with dirty names
test_df <- as.data.frame(matrix(ncol = 6))
names(test_df) <- c("firstName", "ábc@!*", "% successful (2009)",
                    "REPEAT VALUE", "REPEAT VALUE", "")

test_df %>%
  clean_names()
##   first_name abc percent_successful_2009 repeat_value repeat_value_2  x
## 1         NA  NA                      NA           NA             NA NA
test_df %>% 
  clean_names(case = "small_camel")
##   firstName abc percentSuccessful2009 repeatValue repeatValue_2  x
## 1        NA  NA                    NA          NA            NA NA

Compare two or more DFs to see if they have the same types of columns and can be rbound

df1 <- data.frame(a = 1:2, b = c("big", "small"))
df2 <- data.frame(a = 10:12, b = c("medium", "small", "big"), c = 0, stringsAsFactors = TRUE) # here, column b is a factor
df3 <- df1 %>%
  dplyr::mutate(b = as.character(b))

compare_df_cols(df1, df2, df3)
##   column_name       df1     df2       df3
## 1           a   integer integer   integer
## 2           b character  factor character
## 3           c      <NA> numeric      <NA>

tabyls - compare combinations of varibales like xtab

mtcars %>%
  tabyl(gear, cyl)
##  gear 4 6  8
##     3 1 2 12
##     4 8 4  0
##     5 2 1  2

You can “adorn” tabuls with totals, percentages, and more

mtcars %>%
  tabyl(gear, cyl) %>%
  adorn_totals("col") %>%
  adorn_percentages("row") %>%
  adorn_pct_formatting(digits = 2) %>%
  adorn_ns() %>%
  adorn_title()
##              cyl                                    
##  gear          4          6           8        Total
##     3  6.67% (1) 13.33% (2) 80.00% (12) 100.00% (15)
##     4 66.67% (8) 33.33% (4)  0.00%  (0) 100.00% (12)
##     5 40.00% (2) 20.00% (1) 40.00%  (2) 100.00%  (5)

More on tabyls

diffobj - compare two dfs (or other objects) to see what’s changed between them

NB: in RStudio this will open a colorful thing in the Viewer that’s easy to read

library(diffobj)
a <- b <- matrix(1:100, ncol=2)
a <- a[-20,]
b <- b[-45,]
b[c(18, 44)] <- 999
diffPrint(target=a, current=b)
## < a                > b              
## @@ 17,6 @@         @@ 17,7 @@       
## ~       [,1] [,2]  ~       [,1] [,2]
##   [16,]   16   66    [16,]   16   66
##   [17,]   17   67    [17,]   17   67
## < [18,]   18   68  > [18,]  999   68
##   [19,]   19   69    [19,]   19   69
## ~                  > [20,]   20   70
##   [20,]   21   71    [21,]   21   71
##   [21,]   22   72    [22,]   22   72
## @@ 42,6 @@         @@ 43,5 @@       
##   [41,]   42   92    [42,]   42   92
##   [42,]   43   93    [43,]   43   93
## < [43,]   44   94  > [44,]  999   94
## < [44,]   45   95  ~                
##   [45,]   46   96    [45,]   46   96
##   [46,]   47   97    [46,]   47   97
diffChr(letters[1:3], c("a", "B", "c"))
## < letters..  > c("a", ..
## @@ 1,3 @@    @@ 1,3 @@  
##   a            a        
## < b          > B        
##   c            c

Summarizing

skimr::skim() - nice summary statistics for dfs and the like

library(skimr)
iris %>% 
  group_by(Species) %>% 
  skim() %>% 
  print() # print() only necessary so we don't get a kable table in this document
## ── Data Summary ────────────────────────
##                            Values    
## Name                       Piped data
## Number of rows             150       
## Number of columns          5         
## _______________________              
## Column type frequency:               
##   numeric                  4         
## ________________________             
## Group variables            Species   
## 
## ── Variable type: numeric ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
##    skim_variable Species    n_missing complete_rate  mean    sd    p0   p25
##  1 Sepal.Length  setosa             0             1 5.01  0.352   4.3  4.8 
##  2 Sepal.Length  versicolor         0             1 5.94  0.516   4.9  5.6 
##  3 Sepal.Length  virginica          0             1 6.59  0.636   4.9  6.22
##  4 Sepal.Width   setosa             0             1 3.43  0.379   2.3  3.2 
##  5 Sepal.Width   versicolor         0             1 2.77  0.314   2    2.52
##  6 Sepal.Width   virginica          0             1 2.97  0.322   2.2  2.8 
##  7 Petal.Length  setosa             0             1 1.46  0.174   1    1.4 
##  8 Petal.Length  versicolor         0             1 4.26  0.470   3    4   
##  9 Petal.Length  virginica          0             1 5.55  0.552   4.5  5.1 
## 10 Petal.Width   setosa             0             1 0.246 0.105   0.1  0.2 
## 11 Petal.Width   versicolor         0             1 1.33  0.198   1    1.2 
## 12 Petal.Width   virginica          0             1 2.03  0.275   1.4  1.8 
##      p50   p75  p100 hist 
##  1  5     5.2    5.8 ▃▃▇▅▁
##  2  5.9   6.3    7   ▂▇▆▃▃
##  3  6.5   6.9    7.9 ▁▃▇▃▂
##  4  3.4   3.68   4.4 ▁▃▇▅▂
##  5  2.8   3      3.4 ▁▅▆▇▂
##  6  3     3.18   3.8 ▂▆▇▅▁
##  7  1.5   1.58   1.9 ▁▃▇▃▁
##  8  4.35  4.6    5.1 ▂▂▇▇▆
##  9  5.55  5.88   6.9 ▃▇▇▃▂
## 10  0.2   0.3    0.6 ▇▂▂▁▁
## 11  1.3   1.5    1.8 ▅▇▃▆▁
## 12  2     2.3    2.5 ▂▇▆▅▇
iris %>% 
  skim() %>% 
  summary() %>% 
  print() # print() only necessary so we don't get a kable table in this document
## ── Data Summary ────────────────────────
##                            Values    
## Name                       Piped data
## Number of rows             150       
## Number of columns          5         
## _______________________              
## Column type frequency:               
##   factor                   1         
##   numeric                  4         
## ________________________             
## Group variables            None
iris %>% 
  skim(Sepal.Length, Petal.Length) %>%  # tidyselect columns to summarize
  print() # print() only necessary so we don't get a kable table in this document
## ── Data Summary ────────────────────────
##                            Values    
## Name                       Piped data
## Number of rows             150       
## Number of columns          5         
## _______________________              
## Column type frequency:               
##   numeric                  2         
## ________________________             
## Group variables            None      
## 
## ── Variable type: numeric ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
##   skim_variable n_missing complete_rate  mean    sd    p0   p25   p50   p75
## 1 Sepal.Length          0             1  5.84 0.828   4.3   5.1  5.8    6.4
## 2 Petal.Length          0             1  3.76 1.77    1     1.6  4.35   5.1
##    p100 hist 
## 1   7.9 ▆▇▇▅▂
## 2   6.9 ▇▁▆▇▂

You can do cool stuff in the pipeline with it

iris %>%
  skim() %>%
  dplyr::filter(numeric.sd > 1) %>% # "numeric" is the variable type that we're targeting
  print()
## ── Data Summary ────────────────────────
##                            Values    
## Name                       Piped data
## Number of rows             150       
## Number of columns          5         
## _______________________              
## Column type frequency:               
##   numeric                  1         
## ________________________             
## Group variables            None      
## 
## ── Variable type: numeric ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
##   skim_variable n_missing complete_rate  mean    sd    p0   p25   p50   p75
## 1 Petal.Length          0             1  3.76  1.77     1   1.6  4.35   5.1
##    p100 hist 
## 1   6.9 ▇▁▆▇▂

Plotting

Khroma - color scales that are nice and colorblind friendly

https://packages.tesselle.org/khroma/

library(khroma)
bright <- colour("bright")
bright(7)
##      blue       red     green    yellow      cyan    purple      grey 
## "#4477AA" "#EE6677" "#228833" "#CCBB44" "#66CCEE" "#AA3377" "#BBBBBB" 
## attr(,"missing")
## [1] NA
# Preview the theme
plot_scheme(bright(7), colours = TRUE)

Use with ggplot2

data(mpg, package = "ggplot2")

ggplot2::ggplot(data = mpg) +
  ggplot2::aes(x = displ, y = hwy, colour = class) +
  ggplot2::geom_point() +
  scale_colour_bright()

ggiraph - interactive graphs even in Rmd

This can do a whole lot of cool stuff more than this simple example.

More info: https://davidgohel.github.io/ggiraph/

library(ggplot2)
library(ggiraph)
data <- mtcars
data$carname <- row.names(data)

gg_point = ggplot(data = data) +
    geom_point_interactive(aes(x = wt, y = qsec, color = disp,
    tooltip = carname, data_id = carname)) + 
  theme_minimal()

girafe(ggobj = gg_point)

Patchwork - composing multiple plots together

Setting up some plots we can stitch together 
library(patchwork)
library(ggplot2)
p1 <- ggplot(mtcars) + 
  geom_point(aes(mpg, disp)) + 
  ggtitle('Plot 1')

p2 <- ggplot(mtcars) + 
  geom_boxplot(aes(gear, disp, group = gear)) + 
  ggtitle('Plot 2')

p3 <- ggplot(mtcars) + 
  geom_point(aes(hp, wt, colour = mpg)) + 
  ggtitle('Plot 3')

p4 <- ggplot(mtcars) + 
  geom_bar(aes(gear)) + 
  facet_wrap(~cyl) + 
  ggtitle('Plot 4')
p1 + p2 # combine two plots

p1 + p2 + p3 + p4 # by default it makes a square grid, filling rowwise

# plot_layout()
p1 + p2 + p3 + p4 + plot_layout(nrow = 3, byrow = FALSE)

Stacking and packing plots: 
p1 / p2 # / stacks one on top of the other

p1 | (p2 / p3) # | plots side by side.  Note the parens

p1 + p2 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = 'This is a title',
    caption = 'made with patchwork',
    theme = theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 16, hjust = 0.5))
  )

You can define your own layout using letters, with relative sizing 
layout <- "
##BBBB
AACCDD
##CCDD
"
p1 + p2 + p3 + p4 + 
  plot_layout(design = layout)

It gets a lot more complicated and cool. See here for how this package can also avoid plots “jumping” around as you switch slides.

Package and project management

Package development workflow

https://r-pkgs.org: the definitive book by Wickham and Bryan, which explains lots of the stuff below

devtools::load_all() # simulates installing and loading your package
devtools::document() # updates all the documentation and stuff via roxygen2
devtools::check() # run some checks when you think you're ready to release

usethis

# Creating
create_package()
create_project()

# Checking
proj_sitrep() # show wd, proj dir, etc

# Development
use_package() # import packages into DESCRIPTION

# Include important things
use_pipe()
use_tibble()

# Git
use_git()
use_github()
git_sitrep()

Also has git/github functions and other config stuff

Modeling

TidyModels

https://www.tidymodels.org; has a bunch of subpackages, including broom

Data exploration

DataExplorer

https://boxuancui.github.io/DataExplorer/

library(DataExplorer)
plot_bar(ggplot2::diamonds) # Plot frequency of levels in each factor

plot_histogram(ggplot2::diamonds) # Histogram for all continuous variables

plot_boxplot(ggplot2::diamonds, by = "cut")

Stuff to add

(broom)[https://broom.tidymodels.org] to tidy up models

a <- b <- matrix(1:100, ncol=2)
a <- a[-20,]
b <- b[-45,]
b[c(18, 44)] <- 999
diffobj::diffPrint(target=a, current=b)
## < a                > b              
## @@ 17,6 @@         @@ 17,7 @@       
## ~       [,1] [,2]  ~       [,1] [,2]
##   [16,]   16   66    [16,]   16   66
##   [17,]   17   67    [17,]   17   67
## < [18,]   18   68  > [18,]  999   68
##   [19,]   19   69    [19,]   19   69
## ~                  > [20,]   20   70
##   [20,]   21   71    [21,]   21   71
##   [21,]   22   72    [22,]   22   72
## @@ 42,6 @@         @@ 43,5 @@       
##   [41,]   42   92    [42,]   42   92
##   [42,]   43   93    [43,]   43   93
## < [43,]   44   94  > [44,]  999   94
## < [44,]   45   95  ~                
##   [45,]   46   96    [45,]   46   96
##   [46,]   47   97    [46,]   47   97
